content.problemStatement:
You are batting at the pitch and all eyes are on you, the best player in all of IPL!
The opposing team will relentlessly bowl at you an infinite number of balls!

You notice that you only ever score a boundary on the MthM^{th}Mth ball, if NNN modmodmod M≤N/2M ≤ N/2M≤N/2, where NNN is the lucky number of your T-shirt. Now you wanna know how many boundaries will you hit in total?

Formally, you are given a positive integer NNN and you need to find the number of positive integers MMM such that NNN modmodmod M≤N/2M ≤ N/2M≤N/2.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT, which denotes the number of test cases. The description of each test case follows.
The first and the only line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.


Output Format
For each test case, print in a new line, the number of integers MMM which follow the above condition.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of positive integers M that satisfy N mod M ≤ N/2.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=3 every M=1,2,3 satisfies 3 mod M ≤ 1.5, so the count is 3.
N=1 → only M=1 works. N=10 → all M from 1 to 10 work, so answer is 10.
In each case the answer equals N.

solution.approach: For any M ≤ N we have two cases. If M ≤ N/2 then N mod M < M ≤ N/2, so the condition holds. If M > N/2 then floor(N/M)=1 and N mod M = N‑M < N‑N/2 = N/2, so the condition also holds. Hence every M from 1 to N satisfies the inequality. Therefore the answer for each test case is simply N.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used.
